prose: A P0 app crashing issue has been active for 15 consecutive days since 3rd April 2026, with Barclays recording a complaint rate of 12.2% against a peer average of 6.1%, representing a +6.1 percentage point over-index that places this among the most severe stability failures currently visible in the competitive set. The customer evidence rules out isolated device or cache-level faults — users report crashes specifically triggered by card-related functions such as PIN access and card management, suggesting the instability is localised to a discrete feature module or a recent update to card services infrastructure rather than a platform-wide degradation, a pattern consistent with the post-platform-refresh crash cycles seen in HSBC's CHR-003 incident where targeted component failures were misread as general instability and therefore under-prioritised. If this remains unresolved, Barclays faces compounding churn risk among digitally dependent customers who have already demonstrated low tolerance for downtime, alongside material reputational exposure in app store rankings and a credible regulatory scrutiny risk given that sustained inability to access card PIN and account information may constitute a breach of operational resilience expectations under PRA and FCA frameworks.
top_quotes: The app doesn’t even be working and when it’s down, it’s so hard to find support or find a way around it. No one is able to assist you and if you feel like that.
Dreadful. Can't access cards on it, each time I do it keeps crashing and clearing cache does nothing so I can't check my pin numbers or anything in relation to my card nor barclaycard.
chr_resonance: CHR-003 (HSBC 2025): app instability post-platform refresh — crashes and ERR03 errors persisted for weeks.

prose: A P2-severity "Feature Broken" signal has been active for 15 consecutive days since 3 April 2026, with Barclays attracting a 40.9% complaint rate on this issue against a peer average of 36.1%, representing a +4.8 percentage point over-index. Customer evidence consistently points to direct debit management functionality as the primary failure point — the pattern of partial and first-attempt failures across notifications, personal details, and business details updates rules out a single isolated bug and instead implies a systemic issue with write-permission or API transaction handling within the app's account management layer. If left unresolved, Barclays risks accelerating churn among mortgage and business banking customers who depend on direct debit control as a core utility, while the sustained failure to deliver basic self-service functionality creates meaningful exposure to FCA Consumer Duty scrutiny around fair access to financial management tools.
top_quotes: You cant change your mortgage direct debits in any way on here - useless!
Not everything works. Notifications don’t work. Updating personal details don’t work on the first time. Updating business details don’t work on the first time. Cannot cancel direct debits on the app.

prose: Payment failure within the Barclays app has been an active P0 issue for 15 consecutive days since 3rd April 2026, with a complaint rate of 5.9% against a peer average of 5.0%, representing a +0.9 percentage point over-index. The customer evidence rules out isolated user error or device-specific faults, instead pointing to a systemic breakdown in the payment authorisation handoff flow — specifically, the app is failing to complete the redirect loop back after external bank authorisation, combined with mid-session chat and support disruption that prevents customers from resolving failures in real time. If left unresolved, this creates compounding churn risk among customers who are unable to meet recurring financial obligations through the app, while the inability to make scheduled payments carries potential regulatory exposure under FCA Consumer Duty obligations around service reliability and customer harm.

prose: Barclays' technical login failure complaint rate of 3.5% is 4.1 percentage points below the peer average of 7.6%, indicating that customers experience roughly half the authentication friction of those banking with competitors. This reliability at the critical first moment of engagement directly reduces the risk of customers abandoning sessions and seeking alternative providers, reinforcing habitual app usage that deepens primary banking relationships over time.

The customer evidence rules out isolated device or cache issues — users explicitly report cache-clearing providing no relief, with crashes consistently triggered around card-related functions such as PIN access and card management, pointing instead to a regression or instability within a specific feature module, potentially following a recent release or platform change analogous to the HSBC CHR-003 post-refresh instability pattern seen in 2025.
